title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a notice from a local charity asking for volunteers to help with community projects. Write a letter to the charity coordinator explaining why you would like to volunteer, describing any relevant experience or skills you have, and asking for information about the available opportunities.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Coordinator,

I am writing in response to your recent notice seeking volunteers for community projects. I would be very interested in getting involved and believe I could make a useful contribution.

I have always felt strongly about giving back to the community, particularly when it comes to supporting elderly residents. Last year, I spent three months volunteering at a care home near my university, where I organised activities and kept residents company. It was one of the most rewarding experiences of my life.

In terms of skills, I am patient, reliable, and comfortable working with people of all ages and backgrounds. I also have some experience with basic administration tasks, such as answering emails and organising schedules, which might be helpful in your office.

I would be grateful if you could send me more information about the types of projects currently available and the time commitment involved. I am free on weekends and could also help on Wednesday afternoons.

Thank you for the important work your organisation does.

Yours faithfully,
Chloe Martin

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language community magazine:

'We want articles about volunteering. Have you ever volunteered? What did you do and what did you learn from the experience? Would you encourage other young people to volunteer?'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Why Every Young Person Should Volunteer

Volunteering is one of those activities that sounds wonderful in theory but feels like hard work to actually commit to. I used to feel exactly this way — until I tried it myself.

Last summer, I spent four weeks helping to run a free tutoring programme for children from disadvantaged families. My role was to assist with English and maths sessions, and I also helped organise after-school activities. The experience was challenging at times, but completely transformative.

What surprised me most was how much I gained personally. I developed real patience, improved my communication skills, and came away with a much deeper understanding of social inequality in my own city. I also made friends — both with other volunteers and with the families involved.

Beyond personal development, volunteering gives young people something that a CV course cannot: genuine purpose. Knowing that your time has made a real difference to someone's life is an extraordinary feeling.

If you are hesitating, stop waiting and start now. You have far more to offer than you think — and far more to gain.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your friend Sam, who lives in another country, has asked for your advice about doing voluntary work abroad. Sam wants to know whether it is a good idea, what kinds of programmes exist, and any advice you can give about preparing for the experience.

Write your email to Sam.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Sam,

What a great idea! Volunteering abroad is one of the most enriching things you can do, and I'm sure it would be an amazing experience for you.

There are so many different types of programmes available. You could teach English in a local school, help with conservation projects in national parks, work with NGOs in healthcare, or assist with building projects in rural communities. Websites like VolunteerWorld and Projects Abroad are great places to start your search.

As for whether it's a good idea — absolutely yes, as long as you choose a programme carefully. Make sure the organisation is reputable and that your work genuinely helps the local community rather than taking jobs away from local people. Read reviews from previous volunteers before committing.

In terms of preparation, I would suggest learning at least some basic phrases in the local language. Also, research the culture so you can show respect from day one. And pack light — you won't need as much as you think!

I'm so excited for you. Let me know how it goes!

Warm wishes,
Pablo
